tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file defines a set of boolean filters (Condition objects) that inspect the current Application state (via get_app()) and report properties such as focus, editing mode, selection state, completions, read-only state, etc. These filters are used throughout prompt_toolkit to enable/disable key bindings and UI behavior.

### Architecture
- The module imports get_app() to read the active Application instance and defines many small functions decorated with @Condition (or returning a Condition) that each return a boolean computed from the Application state.
- Most top-level functions are simple predicates that directly query get_app().current_buffer or get_app().layout or get_app().vi_state, etc.
- A few factories create Condition functions dynamically: e.g., has_focus(value) returns a Condition that captures the provided value; in_editing_mode(editing_mode) returns a memoized Condition function for the requested EditingMode.
- The module groups Vi- and Emacs-related predicates for selecting which key-binding sets and modes are active.
- The code is primarily functional (no classes are defined here); the @Condition decorator converts these functions into filter objects used by key binding and layout code.

### Functions & Classes
Note: I preserve exact function names as in the source. For functions that define nested functions, I describe those nested functions and their role.

- has_focus(value: FocusableElement) -> Condition — Factory that returns a Condition which is True when the given value has focus. Importance: generic focus test used for creating filters bound to a particular buffer, UIControl, Window, or container.
  - test() — several variants of a nested function named test are defined depending on the type of `value`. Each variant compares get_app().current_buffer.name, get_app().current_buffer, get_app().layout.current_control, get_app().layout.current_window, or walks a container to see if any Window equals current_window.
  - has_focus_filter() -> bool — the Condition-wrapped function returned by has_focus that calls the appropriate test() variant and is what consumers use.

- buffer_has_focus() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().layout.buffer_has_focus. Importance: quick check if the currently focused control is a BufferControl.

- has_selection() -> bool — Condition. Returns bool(get_app().current_buffer.selection_state). Importance: enable when current buffer has an active selection.

- has_suggestion() -> bool — Condition. Retrieves buffer = get_app().current_buffer and returns buffer.suggestion is not None and buffer.suggestion.text != "". Importance: used to show or use inline suggestion behavior.

- has_completions() -> bool — Condition. Examines current_buffer.complete_state to see if completions are present (state is not None and len(state.completions) > 0). Importance: used to enable completion-specific key bindings or UI.

- completion_is_selected() -> bool — Condition. Checks if complete_state is not None and complete_state.current_completion is not None. Importance: checks whether a completion is actively selected by the user.

- is_read_only() -> bool — Condition.
  - Exact line calling read_only(): return get_app().current_buffer.read_only()
  - Description: Returns True when the current buffer is read only. Importance: central predicate representing buffer read-only status; directly calls get_app().current_buffer.read_only().

- is_multiline() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().current_buffer.multiline(). Importance: detect multi-line input buffers.

- has_validation_error() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().current_buffer.validation_error is not None. Importance: used to indicate validation failure in input.

- has_arg() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().key_processor.arg is not None. Importance: checks whether an argument (numeric prefix) was provided to a binding.

- is_done() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().is_done. Importance: indicates CLI lifecycle termination states.

- renderer_height_is_known() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().renderer.height_is_known. Importance: used to avoid drawing bottom toolbars until terminal size is known.

- in_editing_mode(editing_mode: EditingMode) -> Condition — memoized factory.
  - in_editing_mode_filter() -> bool — nested Condition that returns get_app().editing_mode == editing_mode.
  - Importance: returns a Condition for a specific EditingMode; memoized so repeated requests with same EditingMode return the same Condition.

- in_paste_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().paste_mode(). Importance: detect paste-mode state.

- vi_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().editing_mode == EditingMode.VI. Importance: top-level check for Vi editing mode.

- vi_navigation_mode() -> bool — Condition.
  - Code block (relevant read_only call):
    - obtains app = get_app()
    - initial guard conditions:
      if (
          app.editing_mode != EditingMode.VI
          or app.vi_state.operator_func
          or app.vi_state.waiting_for_digraph
          or app.current_buffer.selection_state
      ):
          return False
    - final return condition (includes read_only):
      return (
          app.vi_state.input_mode == InputMode.NAVIGATION
          or app.vi_state.temporary_navigation_mode
          or app.current_buffer.read_only()
      )
  - Exact line calling read_only(): app.current_buffer.read_only()
  - Importance: determines whether Vi "navigation" key bindings are active; treats a read-only buffer as equivalent to navigation mode (hence the read_only() check is part of enabling navigation bindings).

- vi_insert_mode() -> bool — Condition.
  - Code block (relevant read_only call):
    - obtains app = get_app()
    - guard conditions include:
      or app.current_buffer.read_only()
    - final return: app.vi_state.input_mode == InputMode.INSERT
  - Exact line calling read_only(): app.current_buffer.read_only()
  - Importance: enables Vi insert mode only when not read-only and other guards pass. The read_only() guard prevents insert-mode bindings from activating in read-only buffers.

- vi_insert_multiple_mode() -> bool — Condition.
  - Similar structure to vi_insert_mode; includes read-only in guards.
  - Exact line calling read_only(): app.current_buffer.read_only()
  - Importance: specific to Vi's INSERT_MULTIPLE mode; prevents activation when buffer is read-only.

- vi_replace_mode() -> bool — Condition.
  - Similar structure; includes read-only in guards.
  - Exact line calling read_only(): app.current_buffer.read_only()
  - Final return checks app.vi_state.input_mode == InputMode.REPLACE
  - Importance: controls Vi replace-mode activation; read_only prevents replace mode in read-only buffers.

- vi_replace_single_mode() -> bool — Condition.
  - Similar structure; includes read-only in guards.
  - Exact line calling read_only(): app.current_buffer.read_only()
  - Final return checks app.vi_state.input_mode == InputMode.REPLACE_SINGLE
  - Importance: controls Vi replace-single-mode activation; read_only prevents it in read-only buffers.

- vi_selection_mode() -> bool — Condition. Checks editing_mode is VI then returns bool(app.current_buffer.selection_state). Importance: detect Vi selection mode.

- vi_waiting_for_text_object_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns app.vi_state.operator_func is not None (after verifying VI mode). Importance: detect state when Vi operator is waiting for a text object.

- vi_digraph_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns app.vi_state.waiting_for_digraph (when VI mode). Importance: indicates digraph composing mode.

- vi_recording_macro() -> bool — Condition. Returns app.vi_state.recording_register is not None (when VI mode). Importance: indicates Vi macro recording.

- emacs_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().editing_mode == EditingMode.EMACS. Importance: top-level Emacs-mode check.

- emacs_insert_mode() -> bool — Condition.
  - Code block (relevant read_only call):
    - obtains app = get_app()
    - guard condition:
      if (
          app.editing_mode != EditingMode.EMACS
          or app.current_buffer.selection_state
          or app.current_buffer.read_only()
      ):
          return False
    - returns True otherwise
  - Exact line calling read_only(): app.current_buffer.read_only()
  - Importance: enables Emacs insert-mode only when editing_mode is EMACS, no selection, and buffer is not read-only. read_only() prevents insert-mode in read-only buffers.

- emacs_selection_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns bool(app.editing_mode == EditingMode.EMACS and app.current_buffer.selection_state). Importance: detect Emacs selection mode.

- shift_selection_mode() -> bool — Condition. Returns bool(app.current_buffer.selection_state and app.current_buffer.selection_state.shift_mode). Importance: detect shift-selection state in the buffer.

- is_searching() -> bool — Condition. Returns app.layout.is_searching. Importance: indicates whether global searching UI is active.

- control_is_searchable() -> bool — Condition. Checks current UIControl is a BufferControl and that control.search_buffer_control is not None. Importance: determine whether current control supports searching.

- vi_search_direction_reversed() -> bool — Condition. Returns get_app().reverse_vi_search_direction(). Importance: indicates whether Vi search direction mapping has been reversed.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
Agent's goal: "Read the file to identify exact lines calling read_only() and to propose fixes" and agent expects "All functions that call current_buffer.read_only() and their code blocks."

I identified the exact functions that call current_buffer.read_only() and provided their code contexts and the exact expressions used. The occurrences are:

- is_read_only(): return get_app().current_buffer.read_only()
- vi_navigation_mode(): app.current_buffer.read_only() used in the final return expression
- vi_insert_mode(): app.current_buffer.read_only() used as a guard (part of the if ... return False)
- vi_insert_multiple_mode(): app.current_buffer.read_only() used as a guard
- vi_replace_mode(): app.current_buffer.read_only() used as a guard
- vi_replace_single_mode(): app.current_buffer.read_only() used as a guard
- emacs_insert_mode(): app.current_buffer.read_only() used as a guard

These are the only direct calls to current_buffer.read_only() in this file. The included code snippets above show the exact surrounding conditionals where read_only() is checked, which should help you propose the required fixes (for example, changing calls to a property access like current_buffer.read_only or adapting to a new API signature returning a boolean attribute rather than a callable).

output.pr_title: Fixes bug whereby read_only bool is called as a function
output.pr_body:
This is a simple bug fix to remedy the following issue:

```python
  File ".../site-packages/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py", line 374, in emacs_insert_mode
    or app.current_buffer.read_only()
       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Exception 'bool' object is not callable
```

It would appear to be a simple typo, calling the boolean `read_only` property as if it were a function.
output.masca_optimized:
Summary (MASCA) focused on this PR:

- Motivation / Root cause:
  - Several predicate filters in src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py call current_buffer.read_only() as if read_only is a callable. If read_only is a plain bool/property on Buffer (or any object returned by current_buffer), this leads to "'bool' object is not callable" exceptions.

- Scope / Impact:
  - The only file that directly calls current_buffer.read_only() is src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py. The affected predicates are used throughout the application to select active key-binding sets and UI behavior (Vi/Emacs mode detection). If read_only is a bool on a Buffer, several mode checks will raise an error and break runtime behavior.

- Observations from codebase:
  - Buffer (src/prompt_toolkit/buffer.py) historically exposes read_only as a filter callable (self.read_only = to_filter(read_only)), so calling read_only() is valid for older Buffer implementations. However, the reported exception suggests in some usage read_only is a boolean attribute/property (not callable). To be robust and backward compatible across versions/implementations, filter code must accept either callable or boolean values.

- Proposed fix:
  - Make the predicates tolerant to both callables and plain booleans for read_only. Introduce a tiny helper function in src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py, e.g.:

    def _is_read_only(buffer):
        val = buffer.read_only
        return val() if callable(val) else bool(val)

  - Replace all direct calls to current_buffer.read_only() with _is_read_only(get_app().current_buffer) or similar local usage.

- Risk analysis:
  - Minimal. Change is localized to one module and only affects boolean checks. Keeps backwards compatibility with older Buffer implementations that provide read_only as a callable.

- Tests / Validation:
  - Add unit tests that assert the predicates behave the same when buffer.read_only is a callable returning True/False and when it is a plain bool True/False. Specifically test is_read_only(), emacs_insert_mode(), vi_insert_mode(), vi_navigation_mode(), vi_insert_multiple_mode(), vi_replace_mode(), vi_replace_single_mode().

- Implementation steps:
  1. Add helper _buffer_read_only(buffer) at top of src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py.
  2. Update each predicate that currently uses app.current_buffer.read_only() to use the helper.
  3. Run existing tests and add tests for both callable and boolean read_only cases.

- Files/functions to change (concise):
  - File: src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py
    - Functions: is_read_only, vi_navigation_mode, vi_insert_mode, vi_insert_multiple_mode, vi_replace_mode, vi_replace_single_mode, emacs_insert_mode

This MASCA is optimized to guide a patch that makes read-only checks robust to both callable and boolean read_only APIs with minimal, localized edits and low risk.

functions_to_modify.reason: Currently returns get_app().current_buffer.read_only() — this assumes read_only is callable. Replace with a safe helper that handles both callable and bool cases.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Summary (MASCA) focused on this PR:

To be robust and backward compatible across versions/implementations, filter code must accept either callable or boolean values.

- Proposed fix:

    def _is_read_only(buffer):
        val = buffer.read_only
        return val() if callable(val) else bool(val)

  - Replace all direct calls to current_buffer.read_only() with _is_read_only(get_app().current_buffer) or similar local usage.

- Risk analysis:
  - Minimal. Change is localized to one module and only affects boolean checks. Keeps backwards compatibility with older Buffer implementations that provide read_only as a callable.

- Tests / Validation:

Specifically test is_read_only(), emacs_insert_mode(), vi_insert_mode(), vi_navigation_mode(), vi_insert_multiple_mode(), vi_replace_mode(), vi_replace_single_mode().

- Implementation steps:
  1. Add helper _buffer_read_only(buffer) at top of src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py.
  2. Update each predicate that currently uses app.current_buffer.read_only() to use the helper.
  3. Run existing tests and add tests for both callable and boolean read_only cases.

- Files/functions to change (concise):
  - File: src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py
    - Functions: is_read_only, vi_navigation_mode, vi_insert_mode, vi_insert_multiple_mode, vi_replace_mode, vi_replace_single_mode, emacs_insert_mode

This MASCA is optimized to guide a patch that makes read-only checks robust to both callable and boolean read_only APIs with minimal, localized edits and low risk.

## Files to Modify

## Functions to Modify
- is_read_only (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): Currently returns get_app().current_buffer.read_only() — this assumes read_only is callable. Replace with a safe helper that handles both callable and bool cases.
- vi_navigation_mode (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): Uses app.current_buffer.read_only() in its return expression. Replace the call with the helper to avoid 'bool' object is not callable exceptions.
- vi_insert_mode (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): Has a guard that includes app.current_buffer.read_only(). Change to use the helper so guard works when read_only is a bool.
- vi_insert_multiple_mode (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): Has a guard that includes app.current_buffer.read_only(). Change to use the helper so guard works when read_only is a bool.
- vi_replace_mode (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): Has a guard that includes app.current_buffer.read_only(). Change to use the helper so guard works when read_only is a bool.
- vi_replace_single_mode (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): Has a guard that includes app.current_buffer.read_only(). Change to use the helper so guard works when read_only is a bool.
- emacs_insert_mode (src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py): The reported exception occurs here. The guard uses app.current_buffer.read_only() causing 'bool' object is not callable when read_only is a boolean. Replace with helper to handle both modes.

## Analysis Summary
Root cause: module src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py calls current_buffer.read_only() assuming read_only is callable. In some contexts/buffer implementations read_only is a boolean attribute/property, causing "'bool' object is not callable" exceptions. Fix: make predicates robust by using a small helper that checks if buffer.read_only is callable — if so call it; otherwise coerce to bool. This change is localized to src/prompt_toolkit/filters/app.py and affects a small set of predicate functions used for mode and behavior detection. Tests should validate both callable and boolean read_only cases to ensure backward compatibility.
